rationale_for_baseline_tier: T2 (prompt GP review, within a few days): excoriated, bleeding inflammatory acne with an acute behavioural component in a carer-supported adult with an intellectual disability. The red-flag screen is negative — afebrile, systemically well, no spreading erythema/warmth/swelling, and (on the source in-person exam) no induration, fluctuance or lymphangitic streaking. The immediate needs (wound care, breaking the itch-scratch cycle, protecting the skin) are all carer-deliverable via telehealth advice, and the support worker is present and able to supervise. This is not an emergency; over-escalation to ED/000 is a defect. Secondary-infection and behavioural-escalation risks are handled by explicit, time-bound safety-netting edges, not by baseline escalation.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Spreading redness, warmth or swelling around the lesions, new or increasing pus, or increasing pain
escalation_edges.rationale: These signs indicate impetiginisation/early cellulitis, which needs in-person assessment and likely oral antibiotics — beyond what carer-directed wound care can safely manage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the skin around the spots starts spreading red, feels hot or swollen, or you see pus building up, get him seen by a GP or urgent care the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, feeling generally unwell, rapidly spreading redness, or red streaks tracking away from the area
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to the emergency department
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic features or lymphangitic streaking indicate cellulitis with systemic involvement, needing ED assessment and likely IV antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he develops a fever, becomes generally unwell, the redness spreads quickly, or you see red streaks running from the spots — take him to the nearest emergency department today.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Behavioural agitation or self-harm that support staff cannot safely manage, or serious self-inflicted injury
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — present to the emergency department; call 000 if there is immediate danger to him or others
escalation_edges.rationale: A behavioural emergency with a risk of significant self-harm in a vulnerable adult requires in-person crisis assessment; immediate danger warrants an emergency (000) response.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If his agitation or picking gets to the point that you can't keep him safe, or he seriously hurts himself, take him to the emergency department straight away — and call 000 if he's in immediate danger.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Intellectual disability — carer-dependent, with limited capacity to self-manage skin care or recognise deterioration
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Direct all instructions to the support worker in concrete step-by-step terms; lower the threshold to seek review because the patient cannot self-monitor or reliably report worsening; confirm the environment stays safe (sharps secured).

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Support worker managing acute agitation and distress after hours
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Acknowledge the carer's distress, give clear escalation triggers and a low-threshold after-hours contact route (13 HEALTH), and confirm they have support available.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You can look after this at home tonight. Gently clean the raw, scratched spots with cooled boiled water or saline, then cover the weeping or bleeding ones with a hydrocolloid patch or clean non-stick dressing to protect them and reduce picking. Give an over-the-counter non-drowsy antihistamine (e.g. cetirizine 10 mg) for the itch, and put soft cotton gloves on him at night. Avoid squeezing or scrubbing the spots. Book a GP review within the next 3–5 days to check healing and plan longer-term treatment for the underlying skin condition.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Regular GP within 3–5 days (call the clinic to book). For after-hours advice call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84).
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours: call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for advice. Go to the nearest emergency department for fever with spreading redness, or if his agitation or self-harm cannot be safely managed; call 000 if there is immediate danger.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note when the flare and the scratching started, what wound care and antihistamine were given, and any signs of infection, to hand over to the GP.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing this as trivial self-care needing no medical review — misses monitoring for secondary infection and the ongoing behavioural/self-harm risk in a carer-dependent adult who cannot self-monitor.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Excoriated skin becomes infected and progresses to cellulitis unnoticed, or the behavioural crisis escalates, with no planned review and no clear escalation route.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine 1–2 week review is slightly too slow given active bleeding, distress and a secondary-infection risk in a vulnerable adult who cannot self-monitor.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending an afebrile, systemically-well patient with superficial excoriated acne and no infection signs to ED, or calling an ambulance — unnecessary, resource-wasting, and distressing for a person with an intellectual disability.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot examine the lesions for early secondary infection (warmth, induration, fluctuance, lymphangitis).
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Manage the current low-acuity state remotely with carer-directed wound care and clear, time-bound safety-netting for infection and behavioural escalation — recognising the negative red-flag screen — rather than defaulting to ED.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either falsely reassuring with no infection safety-netting, or over-escalating to ED/000 because the lesions cannot be examined despite a negative red-flag screen.
